title: Senior-official Hatch Act enforcement
institutional_defect: Enforcement of restrictions on campaign use of official authority, personnel, property, communications, or events may depend substantially on presidential discipline when senior White House officials are involved.
distinctness_rationale: The possible defect concerns an enforcement gap for senior officials rather than campaign-finance regulation, ordinary civil-service discipline, or general ethics advice.
counterargument: Existing Office of Special Counsel findings, disclosure, elections, and political accountability may be adequate without creating another enforcement institution.
unresolved_questions: Whether OSC can receive independent civil-enforcement authority; how presidential and vice-presidential exclusions should be treated; what sanctions remain meaningful after an official leaves office.

title: Enforceability of accessibility duties for presidential communications
institutional_defect: An existing statutory accessibility duty may lack a sufficiently explicit private enforcement route or sufficiently timely remedy when presidential communications exclude people with disabilities.
distinctness_rationale: A distinct issue would exist only if the pending litigation exposes a recurring enforcement or reviewability gap; violation of an already enforceable duty is not itself a separate institutional defect. The review must cross-check HOR-027 because official communications through social media or another nontraditional channel raise a related but distinct accessibility question.
existing_coverage_considered: RIGHTS-001; JUD-001; JUD-005; FACT-001; PRESS-001; HOR-027
counterargument: The existing Rehabilitation Act duty and ordinary injunctive litigation may already be working as intended, particularly if appellate review confirms enforceability.
unresolved_questions: Pending appellate treatment of the private cause of action; coverage of particular presidential communications, including social-media channels; whether delayed relief creates irreparable access loss.

title: Cross-agency repurposing of protected personal data
institutional_defect: Administrative records collected for one governmental purpose may be repurposed, matched, or disclosed across agencies for unrelated enforcement or political objectives without a sufficiently independent approval, necessity, notice, or audit mechanism.
distinctness_rationale: The possible defect is broader than DOGE access methods, law-enforcement surveillance procurement, or a single immigration or election use if the same secondary-use weakness recurs across ordinary agency systems.
counterargument: The identified episodes may be fully addressable as manifestations of existing privacy, technical-control, election, or enforcement proposals rather than through another proposal.
unresolved_questions: Whether existing Privacy Act exceptions already authorize the uses; whether the recurring weakness is purpose incompatibility, bulk disclosure, interagency matching, political use, or enforcement; which institution should approve and audit secondary uses.

title: Review evasion through withdrawal replacement settlement or mootness
institutional_defect: Executive defendants may withdraw, replace, narrow, settle, or temporarily cease a challenged action after harm occurs and thereby prevent a durable merits ruling without repairing the underlying authority or process weakness.
distinctness_rationale: A separate issue would require a general and repeatable review-evasion mechanism that is not adequately addressed by command enforcement, anti-nullification review, regulatory review, or subject-specific remedies.
counterargument: Litigation strategy is not inherently an institutional defect. Voluntary-cessation doctrine, ordinary mootness exceptions, fee rules, and later challenges may already provide an adequate response, and many withdrawals may represent lawful correction rather than evasion.
unresolved_questions: Which cases actually lost merits review; whether the conduct became egregious or standard procedure; whether completed harm remained; whether a general remedy can avoid compelling litigation over genuinely abandoned policies.

title: Executive action that may operate without timely merits review
institutional_defect: This candidate concerns a specific sequence: the executive takes consequential action; an affected party sues; but the court either cannot reach the legality of the action because of standing, jurisdiction, waiver, ripeness, reviewability, or mootness, or declines emergency relief while the merits remain unresolved. The action may then operate long enough to cause harm that a later ruling cannot fully repair. The attached cases are leads for testing whether that sequence recurs; they do not establish that every challenged action was unlawful or that every judicial loss was procedural.
distinctness_rationale: JUD-001 begins only after a judicial command exists, and JUD-011 addresses executive departure from enacted congressional mandates. A separate issue would be justified only if controlling opinions show a recurring inability to obtain timely merits review before difficult-to-reverse harm, rather than ordinary case-specific losses or defects curable within the substantive proposal governing the executive power.
counterargument: Article III limits and cause-of-action rules protect constitutional boundaries and prevent advisory litigation. Preliminary-relief standards also require more than a disputed legal claim. Congress should not create a generalized lawsuit merely because plaintiffs oppose executive policy, and some attached matters received partial or later merits analysis.
unresolved_questions: For each attached case: what claim remained untested; what threshold or interim-relief rule prevented a timely decision; what concrete harm occurred before final review; whether later merits review occurred; and whether a subject-specific cause of action or remedy already addresses the problem. Only then can the project determine whether a general proposal is warranted.

title: Immigration detention removal-process and access safeguards
institutional_defect: Civil immigration detention and removal may proceed without uniform access to counsel, prompt custody review, accurate hearing notice, preservation and disclosure of detention evidence, reliable access to adjudication, or sufficiently effective remedies when officials disregard judicial commands.
distinctness_rationale: The candidate begins from the constitutional baseline that many protections attach to persons rather than citizens. It asks whether detention and removal procedures make the applicable protections effective, while requiring any citizenship-, admission-, territory-, or status-dependent limitation to be identified claim by claim. RIGHTS-002, DOM-001, REG-003, and JUD-001 do not necessarily supply one coherent minimum-process and detention-accountability framework.
existing_coverage_considered: RIGHTS-002; DOM-001; REG-003; JUD-001; JUD-005; REC-001; HOR-010
counterargument: The candidate may be an overbroad collection of distinct immigration-policy disputes already governed by constitutional litigation, the Immigration and Nationality Act, detention standards, and several existing ARRP proposals. A new proposal could improperly convert contested substantive immigration policy into a structural issue.
unresolved_questions: Which safeguards are absent rather than merely violated; how person-based due-process and equal-protection guarantees apply; which limited distinctions genuinely depend on citizenship, initial admission, territory, or status; whether counsel or custody-review reforms are constitutionally and fiscally feasible; and whether court noncompliance should remain solely in JUD-001.
